1: A prayer of Habakkuk the prophet, set to Shigionoth — shigionoth (plural) may indicate a dithyrambic, passionately irregular song form; the singular shiggayon appears in Ps 7's superscription. Ch3 is formally a psalm — with Selah pause markers at vv3, 9, 13 and a liturgical instruction at v19 ('for the choir director: on stringed instruments') — appended to the prophetic dialogue. The formal shift from dialogic oracle to liturgical hymn marks the book's resolution: the theodicy of faith culminates not in argument but in worship. Habakkuk enters the argument through the mouth of the complaint-prophet (Habakkuk 1-2) and exits through the mouth of the praise-psalmist (Habakkuk 3).
2: O LORD, I have heard your report and stood in awe; in the midst of the years, revive your work — shim'akha (your report, what I have heard of you) = the content of Habakkuk 1-2 now received; the hearing generates yare'ti (reverential awe). Chayyeh po'alkha bekerev shanim (revive your work in the midst of the years) — the Exodus-conquest theophany (described in vv3-15) is the template: the prophet prays for that divine pattern to recur in history. Beshinah rachem (in wrath remember mercy) — not a cancellation of wrath but its tempering by covenant chesed; the same dynamic as Exodus 34:6-7 (YHWH merciful and slow to anger, yet not clearing the guilty). This phrase became the primary Habakkuk text in Jewish liturgy for the Days of Awe (Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur).
3: God came from Teman; the Holy One came from Mount Paran — the theophany locates YHWH approaching from the south/southeast: Teman = Edomite territory; Paran = the Sinai wilderness. This is the traditional direction of the Exodus theophany: Deuteronomy 33:2 ('the LORD came from Sinai, rose from Seir, shone forth from Mount Paran'); Judges 5:4-5 (Deborah's Song: 'when you marched from the field of Edom, the earth trembled'). The cosmic Warrior-God tradition in which YHWH advances from his Sinai base is one of the oldest forms in Israelite poetry — pre-monarchic, deeply embedded in the wilderness period's memory. Selah invites pause on this primordial approach.
4: His radiance shone like light; rays of brightness came from his hand; and there was the hiding place of his power — qarnayim miyado lo: the qarnayim (rays/horns of light) is the same root as Exodus 34:29-30, where Moses's face 'sent out rays' (qaran) after encountering YHWH. The divine radiance that made Moses's face shine and terrified Israel at Sinai is here the universal light of the cosmic theophany. Vesham chebyonezo (and there was the hiding place of his power) — the paradox: the most visible aspect of YHWH (the light) conceals his deepest power; the brightness is veil as much as disclosure.
5: Pestilence marched before him, and burning plague went out at his feet — dever (pestilence/plague) and reshef (burning plague, pestilence-fire) are the divine warrior's heralds. In Canaanite mythology Reshef was the god of plague and war; here demythologized into YHWH's attendants. The Exodus plagues (Exod 7-12) are the historical referent: pestilence was the covenant instrument against Egypt. YHWH's cosmic march in Habakkuk 3 recapitulates the Exodus on a universal scale — the plagues that struck Egypt are now the standard advance of the divine army.
6: He stopped and shook the earth; with a look he scattered the nations; the ancient mountains crumbled — amad vayemoded eretz (he stood and measured/shook the earth): madad can mean both 'to measure' (surveying) and 'to shake.' Vayater goyim (he scattered the nations) — cf. Psalms 68:1 ('let God arise, let his enemies be scattered'). Harerei ad (ancient mountains) and givot olam (eternal hills) — the most stable features of the physical world become unstable at the divine approach. The geological permanence that ANE peoples associated with divine foundations (mountains were the seats of gods) yields before the Creator who established them.
7: I saw the tents of Cushan in distress; the tent-curtains of the land of Midian trembled — Cushan (likely south Arabian or Nubian) and Midian (south of Judah, east of Sinai) represent the surrounding nations who witness and tremble at the theophany. Their trembling at a God who is not their own demonstrates YHWH's universal sovereignty — not merely a regional deity. Midian appears in the Exodus tradition as the region of Moses's calling (Exod 3) and the initial Sinai theophanies; their trembling confirms this Habakkuk 3 theophany recapitulates the Exodus event.
8: Were you angry at the rivers, O LORD? Was your wrath against the rivers, or your fury against the sea? — the rhetorical questions invoke the Exodus traditions: the Reed Sea crossing (Exod 14-15), the Jordan crossing (Josh 3-4). The waters/sea (yam) in the OT cosmic framework represent both literal waters and the primordial chaos (Genesis 1:2; Psalms 89:9-10). YHWH's action against the waters was not anger at the waters per se but defense of his people — the purpose clause follows immediately. Tirkav al-susekha markevotekha yeshu'ah (you mounted your horses, your chariots of salvation) — YHWH as divine charioteer, the ANE divine-warrior image at its most concentrated.
9: Your bow was unsheathed and made bare; your sworn oaths to the tribes stood firm — shevu'ot mattot omer selah: 'your sworn oaths (shevu'ot) to the tribes (mattot)' — the covenant oaths to the patriarchal tribes (Gen 15; Habakkuk 22:16-18; Habakkuk 26:3; Habakkuk 28:13-15) are the theological ground for the Exodus-conquest. YHWH's military action on Israel's behalf is covenant-oath fulfillment, not arbitrary favor. Tivqa aretz neharot (you split the earth open with rivers) — the water-splitting power now turns from sea-parting (Exod 14) to river-opening; the same sovereign who controlled the Reed Sea controls all water systems.
10: The mountains saw you and convulsed; the floodwaters swept past; the deep roared out its voice — ra'ukha yachilu harim (the mountains writhed in labor-pain/fear) — the birth-agony metaphor for mountains suggests cosmic upheaval reaching to creation's foundations. Tehom natan qolo (the deep [tehom] gave its voice) — the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2 that YHWH subdued at creation speaks again in response to the theophany; the same force that threatened chaos acknowledges the Creator's passage. The cosmic acknowledgment confirms that the divine approach is not merely natural phenomena but the Creator exercising original creative authority.
11: The sun and moon stood still in their places at the gleam of your flying arrows — shemesh yareach amad zevulah: the primary allusion is Joshua 10:12-13 (Joshua's long day at Gibeon: 'Sun, stand still at Gibeon, and moon, in the Valley of Aijalon'). The astronomical bodies halted as light from YHWH's own weapons replaced solar illumination. The theophany in Habakkuk recapitulates the conquest battles where cosmic forces were enlisted in YHWH's war — confirming that the coming divine action against Babylon will be as interventionist as the original conquest of Canaan.
12: In your fury you strode through the earth; in your anger you trampled the nations — bezan titz'ad eretz bezaaf tashresh goyim: the divine march through the earth is simultaneously a judgment-march. The nations underfoot in the theophany are the historical enemies of Israel (Egypt, Canaan) whose defeat the theophany recapitulates — and by implication Babylon, whose defeat the prophet anticipates. The scope (earth, nations) contrasts with the specific purpose in v13 (your people, your anointed one): cosmic action for covenant-particular salvation.
13: You went out to save your people, to save your anointed one — yatza'ta leshaya' ammekha leshaya' et-meshichekha: the entire cosmic theophany of vv3-12 has one purpose: salvation of the covenant people and the anointed one. Meshichekha (your anointed one) may refer to Israel as the corporate anointed, to the Davidic king, or proleptically to the messianic figure. Machatzta rosh mibbeit rasha' (you struck the head from the wicked household) — decapitation imagery for the empire (Egypt, Canaan, Babylon) whose power is broken at the top. The 'wicked household' whose head is removed is the specific counterpart to 'your people' who are saved.
14: You pierced the head of his warlords with their own weapons — naqavta vematav rosh: the ironic judgment pattern in which the enemy's own weapons become the instrument of destruction — the same irony as Haman executed on Mordecai's gallows (Esther 7:10), David beheading Goliath with the giant's own sword (1 Samuel 17:51). Yelaku liseroteri la'akol anavim bamister (they charged to scatter us, exulting as if to devour the poor in secret) — the oppressor's confidence in secrecy and surprise is exposed; the cosmic warrior sees and acts against what the empire thought it could do unobserved.
15: You trod through the sea with your horses, churning through great mounds of water — the final theophany image returns to the sea: darekhta vayam susekha (you trod with your horses through the sea). YHWH did not merely part the water at the Reed Sea but charged through it with his cavalry — the active-warrior image contrasting with the passive 'waters parted' narrative. Chomer mayim rabbim (great mounds/heap of water) — the walls of water at the Reed Sea parting (Exodus 14:22: 'the waters were a wall to them on their right and left'). The Exodus event is the anchor for all the theophany imagery in Habakkuk 3.
16: When I heard, my whole body trembled; at the sound my lips quivered; rottenness crept into my bones — the prophet's somatic response to the theophany; the physical body registers what the intellect has received. Veteqev yavi' batzamai (rottenness entered my bones) — complete dissolution of bodily confidence; cf. Daniel 10:8 (Daniel's strength left him at the vision), Ezekiel 3:14-15 (Ezekiel overwhelmed). Asher anuach leyom tzarah (that I must wait quietly for the day of trouble) — the resolution: waiting posture (nuach, rest/quiet) for the coming Babylonian invasion, now understood as within YHWH's sovereign plan. This is the emunah of 2:4 expressed as embodied waiting-posture under physical terror.
17: Though the fig tree does not blossom and there is no fruit on the vine; though the olive harvest fails and the fields produce no food — the most comprehensive statement of covenantal faith in the OT. Habakkuk lists every element of the ANE subsistence economy: te'enah (fig tree), gefen (vine), ma'aseh zayt (olive yield), sadot (fields, grain), miqneh (livestock), baqar (cattle). The six-term list covers every agricultural and pastoral element of a pre-industrial economy. Total failure of all six simultaneously = worst imaginable catastrophe; the systematic 'though... though... though' accumulation escalates to the point of total deprivation. This is a description of what the Babylonian conquest and subsequent famine would produce — lived experience that threatened to make faith appear absurd.
18: Yet I will exult in the LORD; I will rejoice in the God of my salvation — va'ani baADONAI e'eloze agil be'elohei yish'i: the pivot on va'ani (yet I/but I) is the great faith-declaration of the book. Two verbs of rejoicing: e'eloze (exult, rejoice with leaping) and agil (rejoice with circling/spinning) — active, embodied joy of covenant worship. Elohei yish'i (God of my salvation) — yish'i is the possessive of yeshua' (salvation); the personal appropriation of the salvific God under conditions of total material deprivation. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:47: 'my spirit rejoices in God my Savior') echoes Habakkuk's declaration, applying the same structure to the incarnation as the ultimate form of YHWH's saving presence.
19: The Lord GOD is my strength; he gives me feet like a deer and makes me walk on the heights — ADONAI Elohim cheli: the covenant divine name with the additional Elohim (God-in-power) emphasizes both relational covenant (ADONAI) and sovereign might (Elohim) as the prophet's chayil (strength, army-force). Kachalot raglai (feet like hinds/deer) — the deer traverses mountain terrain inaccessible to human feet; 2 Sam 22:34 and Psalms 18:33 use the same image for David's YHWH-empowered agility. Al bamotai yadrekheni (he makes me walk on my high places) — the mountain-heights are the covenant person's inheritance even when the valleys are stripped bare. The book ends with liturgical instruction ('for the choir director: on stringed instruments'), placing this theodicy-resolution within the community's ongoing worship.
